1.errorText: A woman calls the Tourist Information Centre to ask about cookery classes. The official describes several options. At the Food Studio, classes focus on seasonal products and teach you how to cook and display them. Most people who attend say it's a nice way of getting to know one another. This company offers a discount of 10 percent if you return for a further class. Bond's Cookery School, which opened six months ago, concentrates on teaching healthy food, and focuses on recipes for weight loss. Every Thursday evening they have a free talk at the school at 7.30. The Arretsa Centre specialises mostly in fish dishes, and they also offer a two-hour course in how to use a knife.

signpost.quote: Jonathan Atwell is looking at how a range of animals respond to urban environments. He's found that when they're under stress, their endocrine systems react by reducing the amount of hormones.

4.scene: 情景记忆形成与障碍讲座

signpost.quote: Episodic memories allow us to mentally travel back in time to an event from the past.
opts: Defining the key concept with a memorable metaphor before contrasting it with another memory type

signpost.quote: Unlike episodic memory, semantic memory isn't dependent on recalling personal experiences.
opts: Highlighting a key difference between the two memory types to clarify the concept

signpost.quote: The degree to which you can successfully encode information depends on the level of attention you give to an event while it's actually happening.
opts: Explaining a key factor that determines encoding effectiveness

signpost.quote: Consolidation makes it possible for you to store memories for later retrieval indefinitely. Forming strong memories depends on the frequency with which you try to retrieve them.
opts: Connecting the consolidation process to long-term memory strength through frequency of retrieval

signpost.quote: Research has shown that treatments that improve memory may also have a positive impact on children's social development.
opts: Connecting episodic memory impairment to social difficulties and linking treatment to dual benefits
Concluding the lecture on a general note

At weekends, engineering work means you have to take a bus to Hadham. Linda mentions a new cinema and a good arts centre. She offers to send Matt the name of her doctor in Bridge Street. signpost.quote: Unlike European lizards, which are mostly small, green and fast-moving, sleepy lizards are brown, but what's particularly distinctive about them is the colour of their tongue.
opts: Using contrast with a familiar species to highlight the lizard's distinctive features

signpost.quote: Another threat to their survival isn't a predator at all, but is man-made — quite a large number of sleepy lizards are killed by cars when trying to cross highways.
opts: Introducing an unexpected, non-natural threat after listing predators

signpost.quote: He found that even if they were taken some distance away from their home territory, they could usually find their way back home as long as they could see the sky.
opts: Presenting a surprising finding about navigation method — sky not ground landmarks

signpost.quote: It could be that it's to do with protecting their young — you'd expect them to have a much better chance of survival if they have both parents around.
opts: Introducing the first hypothesis for monogamous behaviour before immediately qualifying it

signpost.quote: One surprising thing we discovered is that there were far fewer meetings between lizards than we expected — it seems that they were actually trying to avoid one another.
opts: Highlighting an unexpected finding from the GPS tracking data

dictation.speaker: LECTURER
parts: Sleepy lizards are brown. What's particularly distinctive is the colour of their
— dark blue, in contrast with the bright pink lining of their mouth.
They have a varied diet including insects and even small animals, but they mostly eat
of varying kinds.
Even though they're large and powerful, they still have predators. Nowadays they're more likely to be caught by
. And quite a large number are killed by cars when trying to cross highways.

Michael Freake investigated the navigation of these lizards. He found that even if they were taken away from their home territory, they could usually find their way back — as long as they could see the
Unlike most animals, sleepy lizards are relatively monogamous — returning to the same
year after year. The male and female stay together both before and after the birth of their young.
However, once the babies have hatched, they have almost no
with their parents — so there's not really evidence to support the idea that monogamy is for protection of the young.
Another suggestion: male lizards in monogamous relationships tend to be bigger and stronger — so maybe the male stays around to give the female
from other males.
In our GPS tracking study, we fixed a tiny device onto the lizards'
and tracked them for twelve days — recording location and even the number of
they took.
Surprisingly, there were far fewer meetings between lizards than expected — they were actively avoiding each other. One hypothesis: male lizards can cause each other serious

4.scene: 咖啡历史与经济社会影响讲座

signpost.quote: In 1522, in the Turkish city of Constantinople, the court physician approved its use as a medicine.
opts: Providing specific historical evidence of early official recognition of coffee

signpost.quote: In 1623 the ruler of Constantinople demanded the destruction of all the coffee houses in the city, although after his death many new ones opened.
opts: Showing that attempts to suppress coffee culture ultimately failed

signpost.quote: In the late 1600s, the Yemeni monopoly on coffee production broke down and coffee production started to spread around the world, helped by European colonisation.
opts: Identifying a turning point that began the globalisation of coffee

signpost.quote: However, coffee prices were not yet low enough for people to drink it regularly at home, so most coffee consumption still took place in public coffee houses.
opts: Noting a key limitation that kept coffee a public/luxury item before introducing a contrasting development

signpost.quote: During this century, production boomed and coffee prices started to fall. This was partly because new types of transportation had been developed which were cheaper and more efficient.
opts: Identifying the 19th-century industrial and transport revolution as the key driver of falling coffee prices

parts: There is evidence that coffee was originally gathered from bushes growing wild in Ethiopia. In 1522, the court physician in Constantinople approved its use as a
By the mid-1500s, coffee bushes were being cultivated in the Yemen. People went to coffee houses to drink and chat. In 1623, the ruler of Constantinople demanded the
of all the coffee houses.
Coffee spread to Europe in the seventeenth century. Coffee shops there performed a similar function to
, providing places for people to meet and discuss the topics of the day.
In the late 1600s, the Yemeni monopoly broke down. Europeans set up coffee plantations in Indonesia and the Caribbean. Coffee types were often named after the
they were shipped from.
In Brazil and the Caribbean, coffee was grown on huge plantations using
. In Java, the Dutch used coffee production as a form of
Coffee prices were not yet low enough for home consumption. In Britain, a new drink was introduced from China —
— which gradually took over from coffee.
But in the USA, after independence from Britain in 1776, coffee remained the preferred drink — as it still is today.
In the 19th century, production boomed and coffee prices fell. Cheaper and more efficient
helped make coffee accessible to working people.
Large parts of Europe were starting to work in industries — sometimes through the
. Coffee as a stimulant became important; it wasn't just a morning drink.
